question_title: Will the Strait of Hormuz 7-day moving average of daily transit calls reach 47 on any day before June 28, 2026?
summary: The Strait of Hormuz is effectively closed with ~5-10 transits/day. Reaching a 7-day moving average of 47 requires sustained traffic at ~50% of pre-crisis levels for an entire week — a massive ramp from current conditions. While a deal signing is scheduled for June 19 and 2000 stranded ships could create surge demand, multiple sequential conditions must be met: deal signed → IRGC compliance (not yet endorsed) → insurance availability (currently frozen) → ships actually transiting in volume. Even under optimistic Monte Carlo simulations, the 7-day MA barely reaches 47 by June 27. The conjunction of all favorable conditions occurring within the 11-day window is estimated at ~6%. Fitch and Metaculus consensus support this assessment, with analysts judging broad reopening unlikely before July.

factors.description: Current traffic at ~5-10 transits/day (5-10% of 94/day baseline). 7-day MA in low single to low double digits. Reaching 47 requires ~6x sustained increase.
IRGC declared 'complete closure' June 10-11 and has NOT endorsed the deal. They operationally control the strait. Without IRGC compliance, no reopening possible.
Insurance markets 'frozen' and 'largely unwilling' to cover Hormuz transit even under military escort. This is the binding constraint on traffic volume — ships can't transit without coverage.
Only 11 days remain (June 16-27). 7-day MA requires SUSTAINED high traffic for a full week. Monte Carlo shows even optimistic ramp scenarios barely reach 47 by June 27.
Deal signing scheduled June 19 in Geneva (75% Metaculus). Deal terms call for 'immediate reopening without tolls.' An electronic MOU reportedly already signed.
~2000 ships stranded near Strait could create a one-time surge if opened. Physical throughput capacity exists (pre-crisis 94/day). Pent-up demand could briefly exceed normal levels.

Fitch assessed broad Hormuz reopening as 'unlikely before July 2026'. Metaculus gives only 40% to any reopening before June 27. This question requires ~50% of baseline capacity, not just any reopening.

reasoning:
**Forecast: 6% (NO is very likely)**

**Key reasoning:**

1. **Massive gap to threshold**: Current traffic is ~5-10 transits/day with a 7-day MA around 8-12. Reaching 47 requires a sustained ~6x increase over a full week.

2. **Timeline is extremely tight**: Only 11 days remain. The 7-day moving average mechanically requires 7 consecutive days of high traffic. Even if the strait "reopens" after the June 19 signing, early low-traffic days drag down the average — Monte Carlo shows even ultra-optimistic ramp scenarios barely clear 47 by June 27.

3. **Multiple sequential conditions required**: Deal signing (75%) → IRGC compliance (unendorsed, ~25%) → insurance availability (currently frozen) → ships physically transiting in volume. The conjunction is low probability.

4. **Fitch and analyst consensus**: "Broad reopening unlikely before July 2026." Metaculus gives only 40% to any reopening before June 27 — and this question requires not just reopening but ~50% of baseline capacity sustained for a week.

5. **Upside scenario**: The 2,000 stranded ships could create a surge if everything aligns perfectly, but insurance is the binding constraint, not physical capacity.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will the Strait of Hormuz 7-day moving average of daily transit calls reach 47 on any day before June 28, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/44080)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, for any single day from June 1, 2026 through June 27, 2026 (inclusive), the 7-day moving average of daily transit calls for the Strait of Hormuz, as reported by IMF PortWatch, reaches 47.0 or higher. It resolves NO if no day in that window shows a 7-day moving average of at least 47.0. The value is read from the IMF PortWatch Port Monitor by selecting the Strait of Hormuz chokepoint and viewing the 'Transit Calls' 7-day moving average series at https://portwatch.imf.org/pages/port-monitor (a 7-day moving average of 47 transit calls resolves as '47'). Because PortWatch updates weekly, the resolver will use the latest available data covering the June 1-27, 2026 window as of June 28, 2026.
fine_print: 'Transit calls' and the '7-day moving average' are as defined and computed by IMF PortWatch for the Strait of Hormuz chokepoint (chokepoint6); a transit call is counted when a ship crosses the chokepoint boundary per AIS data, with re-crossings by the same ship counted only after 48 hours. The threshold is evaluated against the 7-day moving average series, not the raw daily count. If, as of June 28, 2026, IMF PortWatch has not yet published data covering any day through June 27, 2026, the resolver will wait until July 5, 2026 for PortWatch to publish the relevant data and then resolve accordingly. If PortWatch permanently discontinues or fails to publish the Strait of Hormuz transit-call series for the relevant window, the question resolves based on equivalent daily transit-count data from JMIC/UKMTO advisories or other [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) covering the same window, applying the same 47-per-day 7-day-average threshold; if no such data exist, the question resolves N/A.

factors.description: Strait currently at ~5-10 transits/day (5-10% of baseline 94/day). 7-day MA in low single to low double digits. Massive gap to 47 threshold.
IRGC declared 'complete closure' June 10-11 and has NOT endorsed the deal. They operationally control the strait. Without IRGC compliance, no reopening.

Insurance markets 'frozen' and 'largely unwilling' to cover Hormuz transit. Even with security guarantees, underwriters need days-to-weeks to reassess. This is the binding constraint on traffic volume.

Only 11 days remain (June 16-27). 7-day MA requires SUSTAINED high traffic for a full week. Even with instant reopening, early low-traffic days drag down the average. Math shows even optimistic ramps barely reach 47.
Deal signing scheduled June 19 in Geneva (75% Metaculus). Could trigger IRGC compliance and shipping recovery. Deal terms call for 'immediate reopening without tolls.'
~2000 ships stranded near Strait could create a surge if opened. Physical throughput capacity exists (pre-crisis was 94/day). Pent-up demand is massive.
Fitch assessed broad Hormuz reopening as 'unlikely before July 2026'. Metaculus gives only 40% to any reopening before June 27, and this question requires ~50% of baseline capacity.

input.anchor: No historical base rate available - unprecedented situation. Anchor based on decomposition: P(reopening) × P(fast enough ramp) ≈ 40% × 15% ≈ 6%
input.assessment: The question asks whether the 7-day moving average of Strait of Hormuz transit calls can reach 47 (half of pre-crisis ~94/day) by June 27. Currently at ~8/day, this requires a ~6x increase sustained for a week. The deal signing June 19 is the only plausible catalyst, but multiple sequential conditions must be met: deal signed → IRGC complies → insurance available → ships transit in volume. Each step is uncertain, and the conjunction is low probability. Even under ultra-optimistic scenarios, the math barely allows reaching 47 by June 27. Monte Carlo simulation across parameter ranges gives 2-10% depending on assumptions about IRGC compliance probability and ramp speed. The realistic estimate accounting for insurance constraints and Fitch's assessment converges around 5-7%.
input.calibration_notes: Cross-checked against Metaculus 'reopening' question at 40%. This question is strictly harder than reopening - it requires sustained 47+/day volume. P(47 reached | reopening) is perhaps 10-20% given logistics, insurance, and timeline constraints. Monte Carlo range: 2-10% across scenarios.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Whether IRGC will comply with the deal (single biggest uncertainty). 2. How fast insurance markets can respond if IRGC stands down. 3. Whether the 2000 stranded ships could create a short-term surge above 47/day. 4. Whether 'transit calls' in the data include military/escort vessels or only commercial.
input.tool_audit: Research tool: Excellent - provided comprehensive data on the crisis, deal status, prediction markets, and key risks. Monte Carlo simulation: Very useful for testing different scenarios and sensitivity analysis. Metaculus coherence links: Empty (no related questions found via that tool). Metaculus question data: Confirmed no community prediction available for this AIB question.
input.update_triggers: 1. IRGC publicly endorsing the deal would be a major positive signal (+15-20pp). 2. Deal signing failing or being delayed past June 22 would make this essentially impossible. 3. Reports of insurance underwriters offering Hormuz coverage would signal faster recovery. 4. Any military incident or escalation would push probability toward 0%.
input.process_reflection: The research tool was excellent and provided exactly the comprehensive synthesis needed. The sandbox for Monte Carlo was helpful for quantifying the trajectory math and testing sensitivity to key parameters. The main challenge is that this question involves a novel geopolitical scenario with no historical precedent for comparison, making base rates impossible to establish empirically.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

I verified all 7 factors against the full research trace, checked cross-question consistency with 3 related forecasts (#44050, #44056, #44076), searched the worldview store for contradictions, and independently assessed the probability.

### Factor Verification

**Factor 1** (5-10 transits/day, massive gap to 47): GROUNDED. Trace research confirms "3-10 commercial transits per day" and "7-day moving average in the low single digits to low double digits" from Metaculus background and multiple news sources.

**Factor 2** (IRGC declared 'complete closure' June 10-11, hasn't endorsed deal): GROUNDED. Research data explicitly states "On June 10-11, 2026, the IRGC formally declared 'complete closure'" and "The IRGC has NOT publicly endorsed the deal."

**Factor 3** (Insurance markets 'frozen' and 'largely unwilling'): GROUNDED. Research states "Insurance companies remain 'largely unwilling' to cover Hormuz transit, even under military escort."

**Factor 4** (11 days remaining, 7-day MA math): GROUNDED. The Monte Carlo simulations in the trace thoroughly verify the arithmetic. Even the "ultra-optimistic" scenario (50→80/day ramp after signing) doesn't hit 47 on the 7-day MA until June 24-25. The agent's claim that "even optimistic ramps barely reach 47" is verified by code output.

**Factor 5** (Deal signing June 19, 75% Metaculus): GROUNDED. Research confirms Geneva signing ceremony June 19, and the agent's own prior forecast on #44056 was 75%, consistent with the Metaculus figure cited.

**Factor 6** (~2000 stranded ships, pre-crisis 94/day): GROUNDED. Research confirms "~2,000 ships and ~20,000 mariners from 87 countries" and pre-crisis baseline of ~94 transits/day.

**Factor 7** (Fitch "unlikely before July", Metaculus 40% for reopening): GROUNDED. Research states "Fitch assessed broad Hormuz reopening as unlikely before July 2026." Metaculus 40% for #44050 confirmed both by the question data fetch and the agent's own forecast.

### Structural Checks

**Double-counting**: Factors 2, 3, and 7 have overlap — Fitch's and Metaculus's assessments already incorporate IRGC non-compliance and insurance concerns that Factors 2 and 3 address independently. Similarly, Factors 1 and 4 both address the mathematical difficulty from different angles (current gap vs. time constraint). However, the agent explicitly acknowledges this in the reflection: "The reflection shows a large logit gap (3.8) between my factor sum (-6.5) and my tentative estimate (-2.7). This is because the factors are somewhat double-counting." The agent correctly adjusts the final probability upward from the factor-implied ~0.15% to 6%, which is appropriate.

**Wrong-direction factors**: All logit signs are correct. Positive factors (5, 6) push toward YES; negative factors (1, 2, 3, 4, 7) push toward NO.

**Resolution criteria alignment**: The agent correctly identifies that resolution requires the IMF PortWatch 7-day moving average reaching 47.0, not just "reopening." The factors and assessment properly engage with the 7-day MA mechanics, running detailed simulations of daily traffic ramps needed.

**Cross-question consistency**: The agent's forecasts form a coherent hierarchy: 75% deal signing (#44056) → 40% official reopening (#44050) → 6% reaching 47-transit average (#44080). Each conditional step properly reduces probability. The decomposition P(reopening) × P(fast ramp | reopening) ≈ 40% × 15% ≈ 6% is consistent with these related forecasts.

**Worldview store**: The worldview archive entry from May 5, 2026 (iran-war-ceasefire-negotiations) describes a period of even higher tensions (Project Freedom, Fujairah attacks, ceasefire "on the brink"). The situation has since moved toward de-escalation with the June 14 deal announcement, which is properly captured in the current research. No contradictions found.

**Counterargument quality**: Strong. The agent constructs a coherent bullish case around stranded ship surge, pre-signing IRGC compliance, economic pressure on IRGC, rapid insurance turnaround, and faster ramp speeds. It also provides 5 specific conditions that would shift the estimate. This is genuine adversarial reasoning.

**Confidence in estimate (0.55)**: Appropriate given the fast-moving situation, key June 19 signing event still pending, and the agent's explicit acknowledgment of the factor sum gap.

### Probability Assessment

My independent estimate: ~4-7%. The agent's 6% sits comfortably in this range. Key reasoning:
- P(deal signed and effective by ~June 20) ≈ 60-70%
- P(IRGC complies promptly | signed) ≈ 20-30%
- P(traffic ramps fast enough to 47/day avg | compliance) ≈ 15-25%
- Conjunction: ~2-5% via this path, plus ~1-2% for alternative paths (military escort, partial compliance surge)
- Total: ~4-7%

The Monte Carlo simulations ranged from 1.5% (very conservative) to 11% (generous IRGC assumptions), bracketing the agent's 6% estimate. The "realistic" scenario at 2.6% and "base case" at 9.9% average to about 6%.

### Tool Audit

Agent claims "Research tool: Excellent" and reports 8 calls with 0 errors. Ground truth confirms 8 calls, 0.0% error rate. Consistent.

### Conclusion

No hallucinated evidence, no wrong-direction factors, no contradictory assessment, resolution criteria properly engaged, cross-question consistency verified, reasonable counterargument, and the 6% probability is well-supported by both analytical decomposition and Monte Carlo simulation. The double-counting among factors is acknowledged and properly handled through manual adjustment.
